item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have not read or seen Rhinoceros, or had even heard of it, apparently my education is lacking.

item.title: Oh, I get it.
item.content:encoded: I'm dumb, it took me until now (from around the post date) to realize the number 3519 refers to "March 5th, 2019" I don't see scps related to their number very often since they may move around, and this works without it, but neat!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: apologies for the necro, but thanks for the link to 2508. that article and the (sadly unfinished) series of tales attached to it is now yet another one of my favorites, and I would never have found it otherwise since it has hardly any votes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have every right to believe Neil Degrasse was the only survivor

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: BERENGER: I’m the last man left, and I’m staying that way until the end. I’m not capitulating!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The subreddit is having a field day with this today.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What a beautiful day

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's a beautiful day outside. Birds are chirping, flowers are blooming…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No, wait, hold on. How come there’s so many people saying it’s a beautiful day? I thought there was supposed to be only one living person left.

…I mean, uh, it’s a beautiful day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually no, it's not a beautiful day, it's super god damn cold outside, help

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It’s a beautiful day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it's a beautiful day outside.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Happy birthday to me! Everyone’s dead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dude, not yet!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey is the world supposed to end today, I haven't finished my exotic quilt collection yet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Did I ever make a comment on this? I don't think I ever did.

Honestly one of my favorites, not just from 3kon, but on the entire site. The ending line hit me like a bag of bricks the first time I read it, and it hasn't been matched by literally anything on the site since, except maybe the last few lines of SCP-2000 (and even then it's arguably executed better). It contains relevant political and social commentary without being too heavy on it. It's got verisimilitude for days. The setup is nearly flawless, it's not too short but not too long either, it packs solid emotion…

Sirpudding, if you're reading this, please, for the love of God, never delete this article. It's an easy top ten for me; hell, maybe even top five.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 30 minutes til doomsday, wish us luck

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: it’ll be a shame not to be able to read it past tomorrow, a true artist of his time

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's happening tomorrow! I know sirpudding has expressed some reservations about the popularity of this work, but I just want to say I've thoroughly enjoyed it on several read-throughs.

item.title: ...12/7. Terrifying!
item.content:encoded:
After looking through the history of the SCP (cuz ya do), I have found that there is an error: The initial date of the doomsday was december 7th, 2019. Trump made a tweet saying that the aztecs told him world was ending on 12-7. after SirPudding changed it to 3-5-19 (to fit the SCP number, cuz ya do), he never changed the tweet to reflect the new date. CHANGED!

wait… what the fuck? I never did this. There was never a problem…

meh, carry on with your panicking.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God Dammit, I was about to post something about this.

START PANICKING!!!!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So the world did end, but not in a way anyone was expecting.

Great SCP, upvoted. Also, it looks like I chose great time to read this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have every right to believe that Neil deGrass was the only one to survive this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It seems like I got one thing right.

Kellyanne Conway, counselor to the president, has a picture of senior staff being sworn in framed on her office wall. She calls it a reminder of "durability and toughness and longevity" of those who are still there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For whatever it's worth: I can see what Tufto means and why it doesn't work for him. Writing — particularly horror! — is weird, and amounts to kind of a magic trick.

But for me, it was always clear where this was going (I'm reading the article, obviously the world hasn't ended), and so what emerged for me was the horror of seeing so many people justifying their acts of self-destruction over what was ultimately nothing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Rereading this after a year and a half, I have no idea what I was thinking when I originally novoted. This is absolutely amazing. I wish I could give this 100 upvotes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that's different, at least.

FWIW, I never intended much of a twist, rather I was trying to capture my sense of horror at the willingness of people to go along with something like this, and the sense of it feeling both incredibly stupid and inevitable I had in my nightmare.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Flip-flopped for a while, but eventually settled on a downvote. This article is almost entirely dependent on the revelations contained within the twist, but the preceding article is too ambiguous to make the twist work properly. We're already uncertain of whether the apocalypse is real or not by the time the twist comes around, muting its impact and making the article very straight and lacking in tension.

There's good stuff here, it just doesn't fully come together for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Most likely scenarios for human extinction still have life going on in our absence, and even the fall of civilization still likely has survivors living as hunter-gatherers. It's the height of arrogance to be unable to imagine the world without you in it.

History is a tiny part of the life of our species and this species vanishingly small flash in the span of life of Earth. It was, before us, and it will be after. Eventually we will mostly just be an anamolous thin layer of plastics in the geological record.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is the way the world ends; not with a bang, but with a beautiful day.

item.title: Re: You are me, now
item.content:encoded:
I mean judging from the first interview Neil deGrasse Tyson does not seem getting infected.

He's not infected by the early religious and conspiracy theory strains. By the time it mutates into what got Sigrid Soika…

"And if that's the case, it is easy for me to imagine that everything in our lives is just the creation of some other entity for their entertainment. I'm saying, the day we learn that it is true, I will be the only one in the room saying, I'm not surprised,"
— Neil DeGrasse Tyson

So I assumed some percent of population has innate immunity to the infection due to their skepticism or memetic resistance or both.

Sure they absolutely do, but that does not mean they lived. A lot of people died in the nuclear exchange in the Middle East, or from fallout, a lot of people died when essential services failed, a lot of people died from disease when bodies were rotting in the streets, a lot of people were murdered by infected trying to "save" them, and a lot of people who were otherwise immune committed suicide because they couldn't deal with the grief, horror and loneliness.

But Rory Jones being the only survivor was not obvious to me.

Well he's certainly not literally the only survivor. For one thing there are about 100 isolated tribal groups in the world with nearly no contact with other human beings, with a total population of around 10,000. For another, there's the survivors in Worth a Shot. However, the Sentinalese don't have written language, much less access to the Foundation's secret servers, and even the Foundation personnel in that tale aren't read into 3519. There's only one person left from the 3519 research group, and unless there's more O5s, everyone above him is dead so he's the only one who could access the file.

item.title: Re: You are me, now
item.content:encoded:
It occurs to me that the assumed reader here can logically only be JR O5-06 Dr. Rory Jones.

Why though?

I mean judging from the first interview Neil deGrasse Tyson does not seem getting infected.
So I assumed some percent of population has innate immunity to the infection due to their skepticism or memetic resistance or both.

I assumed the majority of humankind died and it's an end of our civilization (unless O5-06 manages to activate SCP-2000 and it works fine). But Rory Jones being the only survivor was not obvious to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A self-fulfilling apocalypse prophesy. The irony is morbidly funny.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1) If it were a tale instead of a mainlist article

If it were it wouldn't have been a valid contest entry. This whole "tales" versus "article" thing is artificial anyway. SCP articles are short stories, just a particular formal kind. This happened to be a story that works within the format.

If the article were more clear about this taking place in a parallel universe, rather than the same one as other mainlist article.

This feels like it would be at minimum unnecessary cruft and at worst unintentional foreshadowing.

Really all articles take place in an alternate universe, having disclaimers to that effect seems redundant.

I tend to view every mainlist article as existing in the same universe unless explicitly stated otherwise

In which case, since that includes SCP-2442, the entire setting is a complex metaphor for one woman's psychological state, and maybe this represents the way she sometimes feels like everyone else is giving up, letting the world end and abandoning her, or perhaps it is her frustration with the conflict between her suicidal ideation and survival instinct. Once you canonize that particular article it perforce reframes the entire universe. Certainly it allows for contradictions in the mainlist, since she is large and contains multitudes.

(Incidentally, I have the exact same problem with 3002 and the newly-posted 4005.)

You should have it with about 75% of Doomcon.

it's entirely possible I'm the only one on the site who has this problem

If you read the discussion, this is the the only cited reason for downvoting, so you aren't alone. I personally disagree, and would never even consider a universal canon, but you do you.

ETA: Ugh wrong link for evers. Embarrassing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this. In my personal headcanon, SCP 3519 seems like it could be a precursor to The Gate Guardian event, which would confirm the date of that ominous final message.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, I want to apologize if it seemed like I was saying that you were ripping off SCP-2317; that wasn't at all my intention, and I'm truly sorry if it came across that way. I just meant that, intentionally or not, this article felt like part of a greater trend which has been growing within the site over the last few years.

Second, I've been thinking about it for a few days, and I've decided I'm going to remove my downvote. The reason I'm removing it is because I figured out what the problem is, and I think it's a purely subjective issue on my part. Details below if you're interested.

+ show block
– hide block


So the thing is, I feel like there are two very small things which would have instantly fixed any and all problems I have with this article:

1) If it were a tale instead of a mainlist article
2) If the article were more clear about this taking place in a parallel universe, rather than the same one as other mainlist articles. (For example: if the 3519 item were an interdimensional artifact with a record of these events contained within, or if there were some note at the beginning stating that this document appeared in the database with no explanation.)

My problem is- and this is the crux of my issue with the article- I tend to view every mainlist article as existing in the same universe unless explicitly stated otherwise (or unless it can be handwaved away, like the contradictory 001 proposals). Yes, yes, I know, there is no canon; maybe mine is a completely irrational viewpoint. I just can't help but feel like the mainlist is some kind of unified narrative; otherwise, I feel like the entire site loses its core. And that's where my subjective issues arise: without a message along those lines, I assume by default that this takes place in the same universe as every other mainlist article; and that would make this a definitive ending to the SCP Foundation as a whole. Every other article on the mainlist is invalidated; none of the other containment procedures matter, because the world ends on March 5th 2019 anyway and the Foundation doesn't manage to stop it. The idea that this is absolutely, definitely how the story of the Foundation ends is not only (unintentionally) presumptive, but just straight-up depressing. (Incidentally, I have the exact same problem with 3002 and the newly-posted 4005.)

Again, this is entirely my own subjective issue, which is why I'm removing the downvote. There are no problems with the writing, and if either of the above changes had been made I would have loved it. And hey, maybe I'm the weird one here; it's entirely possible I'm the only one on the site who has this problem. Maybe with the Wiki's unique take on the concept of canon, viewing the mainlist as a unified narrative is an idiotic viewpoint to take; but nevertheless, it's one I can't help but stick with, and it's the main reason- possibly the sole reason- that this article and others like it rub me the wrong way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can tell you that SCP-2317 had nothing to do with this. The main inspiration for the nightmare that inspired this was probably a radio documentary about The People's Temple that I had listened to a few days beforehand.

The difference between this and most of the other apocalyptic scenarios on the wiki is that there is no external cause here. We do this one to ourselves, and we do it to ourselves on an individual basis.

This isn't a thing that the Foundation could not stop. It is a thing that they failed to stop.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm sorry, but I have to give this article my firmest downvote. My explanation as to why ran a little long, so you can check it out in the collapsible if you don't mind some monologuing.

+ show block
– hide block

I should preface this with the fact that this downvote has very little to do with the quality of the writing (I actually think it's a fairly well-written skip with some cool formatting) and much more to do with the fact that it represents the culmination of a theme which has- at least, from what I've seen- become increasingly pervasive on the site in recent years: the theme of "Fuck you, the entire world is screwed and there's nothing the Foundation can do about it, everyone you know and love is gonna die (or in this case is already dead), you can go to hell." I feel like this trend may have been accidentally kick-started by SCP-2317; honestly, I didn't like the idea in that article either, but as a one-time thing I could accept its existence. But now it feels like every fourth article is some unavoidable apocalypse that the Foundation either has no hope of stopping or (as in the case of 2317 itself) has given up attempting to stop because they think it's impossible.

Don't get me wrong, I understand that a powerful part of horror is the idea that, in the end, there may be nothing we can do to stop the Bad Thing. There's a lot of potential in the idea that the Foundation may ultimately be fighting a losing battle with an anomaly; I'm actually a big fan of SCPs that hint toward the Foundation being utterly unprepared for the next attack. But, at least to me, the idea that they would give up, even under circumstances like this or 2317, flies in the face of everything the Foundation represents. I want to be clear: this is not a rant about horror. This is a rant about despair, and its increasing grip on the site.

Just to be clear, I don't in any way blame sirpudding for this trend; this is just one skip, and- again- a fairly well-written one. This is a general pattern of the community, and honestly it just wears me down. To me, articles like this aren't scary, they're just depressing; and considering that the real world is plenty depressing enough right now, that's the last last thing I need right now. I apologize for ranting, and I wish sirpudding luck in all his(?) future endeavors.

(P.S. I will say, the joke with the Trump tweet was fucking genius. I can unambiguously say I enjoyed that bit.)

item.title: You are me, now
item.content:encoded: It occurs to me that the assumed reader here can logically only be JR O5-06 Dr. Rory Jones. Since he is also my ridiculous Mary Sue wankatar, that's you too reader. So you probably should go downvote yourself.

item.title: Re: +400
item.content:encoded: uses the +400 mark as an opportunity to shill

Oh look, this precious self-insert just became my first article to hit +400. Why can't this asshole die?

Oh yeah, because I made him the ultimate archetypical survivor.

I hate you, O5-6 Rory Jones, I hope you live the rest of your miserable life going insane from isolation in an empty world. I want you to wake up every morning and wonder if you are going to finally kill yourself, but instead decide to keep going because of your meaningless job, Mr. Foundation-of-One. Oh wait, that's exactly what I did to you.

Good.

item.title: Re: Great Story
item.content:encoded:
but it's not a Skip. -1.

Let's see, does it have Special Containment Procedures? Yes, it does. Looks like an SCP article to me.

This isn't even a "format screw" but rather a straight use of the format. In what way is this not an SCP article?

Blowing up the world is only fun once.

I like apocalyptic and post-apocalyptic fiction quite a bit. Blowing up the world will always be fun for me, every time.

item.title: Re: Great Story
item.content:encoded: Just out of curiosity, what about this SCP article disqualifies it as an SCP article for you?

item.title: Great Story
item.content:encoded:
but it's not a Skip. -1.

Blowing up the world is only fun once.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks! I am not sure why this post was a reply though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just fixed a mistake in the T-minus count in the timeline. I assume you were counting backwards from T=0 and mixed up +/- in one spot. You had this (reversed for clarity):

  • 1/19 was T-45
  • 1/17 was T-47
  • 1/15 was T-45 (again)

And then everything before 1/15 was off-by-4.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was spectacular. Even if it's not a demigod, a killer statue or Satan's teddy bear, it's one hell of a Scip. The feeling of dread and inevitability builds a truly terrifying atmosphere. Loved it, and upvoted it.
Post Scriptum: Trump's tweet and the Junior Researcher promoted to O5? Nice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this one. It's very terrifying, has good realistic dialogue, just overall a good scp. Strong +1.

(Also, happy 1 year until the world ends!)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I loved this during the contest.

Coming back now I love it even more. I wish I could upvote again.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, normally I downvote mainlist SCPs that explicitly break the status quo out of principle, but this is done far to well for that. Have an upvote instead.

(Also, this is why we have SCP-2000.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Junior Researcher Dr. Rory Jones promoted to O5-6.

Upvoting

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
1. 2651

2. IJAMEA

3. Daybreak

4. dis

That's not to say this is how I rank them by quality or something, these have just been the stuff that's spoken to me as an individual. They are all very different things and succeed in their own ways, which tends to be the problem with most arbitrary list based stuff.

I saw your deleted scenes; I liked them, especially the follow-up storyline to the secular mutation, which I thought provided a nice example of how the self destructive nature of the virus could also lead to different deathorror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was not my favorite entry for the 3000 contest.

I hear that, nobody was more surprised than I was about the 7th place finish.

That/those title/s undoubtedly go/es to Daybreak, which is, I think, an unfair comparison to any other work we have not only by quality but also because it's thematically bonkers.

Yeah, I thought I was doing pretty good before I read that and saw like everything I was doing, but better. :|

However this is probably the… 3rd or 4th favorite thing that I think the Wiki's done this year, which sounds underwhelming but we've actually had a pretty great one so far.

  1. Daybreak
  2. Best 3000 contest entry
  3. Maybe something else
  4. SCP-3519

?

It's also imo the best straightforward piece on the site, in that the concept alone is somewhat intriguing and disturbing but you manage to elevate it to orbital heights through the use of sheerly excellent writing.

This is some seriously high praise, I may need to add it to the blurbs.

I actually would've preferred just a little bit more on that front, as there's a metric fucktonnage of moving imagery to be derived from that.

I don't know if you've seen but my author page has the cut material.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't believe I never commented on this.

This was not my favorite entry for the 3000 contest. It's also not my favorite ApocalySCP. That/those title/s undoubtedly go/es to Daybreak, which is, I think, an unfair comparison to any other work we have not only by quality but also because it's thematically bonkers. However this is probably the… 3rd or 4th favorite thing that I think the Wiki's done this year, which sounds underwhelming but we've actually had a pretty great one so far. It's also imo the best straightforward piece on the site, in that the concept alone is somewhat intriguing and disturbing but you manage to elevate it to orbital heights through the use of sheerly excellent writing. You make the scariest thing here out to be the reader's own imagination; their terror is our terror. You don't need a reality-destroying demon or a machine god to instill pure fear; all you need is a man.

So yeah if it's not obvious I like to think the ending log is craaazy good. What gets me is that the way the article presents it in a manner that draws on the horror from actual disease - aside from the obvious bureaucratic overtones and jargon as a layman I can easily imagine something like this playing out from the perspective of a CDC guy watching the world go dark. The rawness of it filtered through the log format is like nothing I've ever read before and every time I see those last three lines - especially the supremely cathartic 'T+1' - do not fail to give me chills every single time.

But even as I love all of it I think it would not work nearly as well if the context provided by the previous pieces were not present and available to give the reader a concrete handhold to be ripped away. It needed that kind of grounder that got you down from the perspective of victims, and I actually would've preferred just a little bit more on that front, as there's a metric fucktonnage of moving imagery to be derived from that. Still I think the conciseness works to its benefit only in that it moves the story along - the unrelenting fast pace you manage to convey while still working in a modicum of character development for Rory enabling us to hone in with him end all lead up to a stunning, gripping finale.

when i ask myself 'self, do you think we should upvote this?' i actually don't do that. i ask myself 'self, do you want to support good writing?' and then when he says yes he pushes the upvote button.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, maybe. I think it would work best as the climax of a series, in which Rory is quite old by that point, and the tale at least hints at the idea that 2000 has been corrupted (which is implied in the original) to make Daeva or something instead of humans.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally I think it would be quite an interesting tale series to have Rory activate SCP-2000 single-handedly. Although, that's just me. It's a pretty nice thought to dwell on, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That right there was where my heart broke. Perfect.

item.title: Crosslinked
item.content:encoded: Link to Mobile Task Force Upsilon-4 ("Sugar Pill") added.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well I deliberately wrote this to be SCP-2000 agnostic (and I personally don't especially like SCP-2000). However if it does exist in this version of the Foundation, Rory wouldn't have known about it. He only is cleared for it when he becomes O5 and even then he would need to convince the system to read him into it and then undergo the inoculation for the meme-glyph security wall (without knowing it exists or why he should care). Then of course he needs to remember Yellowstone exists and then cross half the country to get there. Finally, assuming no other survivors made it, he needs to single-handedly activate it and hope the clone replicators don't pump out Daeva or whatever instead of humans. All of this of course while surviving completely on his own.

Even then they aren't out of the woods yet because he would need to make sure that the clones aren't ever exposed to 3519.

Author headcanon is there is no SCP-2000 in this specific Foundation. Although I will also accept the irony of it existing but him never learning about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I guess this is what SCP-2000 is for. Just gotta make sure that guy knows where it is and how to activate it…
